extra.reasoning:
The property requires that in every reachable state, opted_out=true implies sent=false (never opted_out AND sent simultaneously).

Transition t5 [on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks >= 5): sent := true] directly sets sent=true while opted_out is true. This is the violating rule; I only need to show its guard is reachable.

Reaching the guard requires opted_out=true and ticks>=5. ticks is incremented only by t4 (tick), capped at 5. From the initial state (opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0):
1. tick -> ticks=1, sent=false
2. tick -> ticks=2
3. tick -> ticks=3
4. tick -> ticks=4
5. tick -> ticks=5
6. optout (t1) -> opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=5
7. send_attempt -> guard of t5 (opted_out and ticks>=5) holds; t5 sets sent=true.

Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=5 — which violates globally(opted_out implies not sent). This occurs at event 7, within the 9-event bound.

Note on rule interaction: on send_attempt with opted_out=true, both t3 (sent:=false) and t5 (sent:=true) match when ticks>=5. The transition list specifies no priority ordering, so these are alternative enabled transitions and t5 may fire nondeterministically. Since the property must hold on EVERY reachable execution, the existence of this single execution where t5 fires is sufficient to falsify it. The guard for t5 is plainly reachable, so the system as specified is unsafe (the design clearly intends opted_out to suppress sending, but t5 re-enables sending after 5 ticks).
